title: Machine-Generated Email Accounts with Rapid High-Value Card Testing
narrative: Fraudsters deploy bots to create throwaway email accounts with random 12-16 character alphanumeric strings (e.g., 'k7j2x9m4q8w1@gmail.com') and immediately link them to stolen card data for accelerated testing. Unlike human-typed randomness, these emails show no vowel clustering or memorable patterns. The attack unfolds in 15-45 minute windows: 8-15 transactions per email, starting with $1-$5 authorization probes, then jumping to $200-$800 purchases once a card validates. P_emaildomain is consistently free providers (gmail, yahoo, outlook) with account creation timestamps within 2 hours of first transaction. Card velocity shows card1_txn_count_24h spiking to 18-40 across multiple machine-generated emails. Transactions cluster in late-night hours (txn_hour 1-5 AM merchant local time) with dist1 values exceeding 3000km, indicating VPN/proxy use. ProductCD skews heavily toward easily liquidated digital goods or gift cards.
indicators: P_emaildomain username contains 12-16 random alphanumeric characters with no recognizable word fragments or vowel patterns
8-15 transactions per email address within 15-45 minute windows
Transaction amounts escalate from $1-$5 initial probes to $200-$800 purchases after card validation
card1_txn_count_24h reaches 18-40 across multiple newly-created email addresses
85%+ of transactions occur during txn_hour 1-5 AM in merchant timezone
dist1 (distance between billing and IP location) exceeds 3000km for 70%+ of transactions
P_emaildomain account creation timestamp within 120 minutes of first transaction usage
summary: Email addresses with 12-16 random alphanumeric characters (no recognizable patterns) make 8-15 transactions in 15-45 minute bursts, starting with small $1-$5 test charges then jumping to $200-$800 purchases. The same stolen card appears across multiple throwaway emails with 18-40 transactions in 24 hours, concentrated in late-night hours with IP distances exceeding 3000km from billing addresses.
reasoning: Bots are rapidly testing stolen card numbers to identify which ones still work before the cardholder or bank catches on. The escalating transaction amounts, newly-created disposable emails, extreme geographic mismatches, and nocturnal timing indicate automated credential stuffing designed to maximize fraudulent purchases before cards are frozen.
indicator_explanations: Email username looks machine-generated with no human-readable patterns or structure
Email is cycling through numerous transactions in a compressed timeframe
Purchases start small to test card validity then jump to high-dollar amounts
Same card is being tested across many freshly created email accounts
Activity concentrates in overnight hours when monitoring is weakest
Billing address and actual IP location are separated by transcontinental distances
Email account was created moments before being used for transactions
